days.title: The First Line of Code You MUST Write 🤯
days.hook: Stop scrolling! If you want to code, you HAVE to start here.
days.code: print('Hello, World!')
days.explanation: Alright, let’s kick things off! The print function shows text on your screen. Whatever you put inside the quotes gets displayed — just like saying hello to Python!

days.title: Variables: The DNA of Coding 🧬
days.hook: Imagine a box that can hold ANYTHING. That's a variable.
days.code:
name = 'Python'
print(name)
days.explanation: Variables are like boxes that hold information. Here, we stored the word ‘Python’ in a box called name, and print it out!

days.title: Math in Python is INSANE ⚡
days.hook: Throw away your calculator. Python is faster.
days.code:
x = 5
y = 3
print(x + y)
days.explanation: Python can do math too! Add, subtract, multiply, divide — just like your calculator but in code!

days.title: Make Your Code TALK Back 🗣️
days.hook: Want your code to actually listen to you? Watch this.
days.code:
name = input('Enter your name: ')
print('Hello', name)
days.explanation: Want to talk to the user? The input function lets them type something, and you can use it right after — just like saying hello personally!

days.title: The 'Age' Glitch in Python ⚠️
days.hook: Warning! Your age is NOT a number in Python... yet.
days.code:
age = int(input('Age? '))
print(age + 1)
days.explanation: Inputs are text by default! If you don't use 'int()', Python thinks your age is a word. Convert it, or your math will break!

days.title: Merging Words Like Magic 🪄
days.hook: You can literally glue words together in code.
days.code:
first = 'Py'
second = 'thon'
print(first + second)
days.explanation: You can join two strings using a plus sign! It’s like sticking two words together to make a new one.

days.title: The Ultimate String Hack (f-strings) 🔥
days.hook: Stop using plus signs! Do this instead.
days.code:
name = 'Alex'
print(f'Hi {name}')
days.explanation: See that 'f'? It stands for Fast. Or Format. Doesn't matter. It lets you put variables RIGHT inside the text. Clean. Professional.

days.title: How Computers Make Decisions 🧠
days.hook: Your code has a brain. Here is how it thinks.
days.explanation: If statements let you make decisions. If the condition is true, Python runs the code inside — simple logic power!

days.title: The 'Else' Universe 🌌
days.hook: What happens if the answer is NO?
days.explanation: If something isn’t true, the else block runs instead. It’s like saying: if not this, then that!

days.title: Grading System in 3 Lines 📝
days.hook: I built a teacher in 3 lines of code.
days.explanation: Elif means ‘else if’. You can check multiple conditions one after another — perfect for grading systems!

days.title: Lists: The Super Variable 📦
days.hook: Why have 10 variables when you can have 1?
days.code:
fruits = ['apple', 'nbna']
print(fruits[0])
days.explanation: A List is a backpack. Throw anything in it. Pull it out by its number. Index 0 is the first item. Always.

days.title: Infinite List Glitch? ♾️
days.hook: You can add items to lists forever.
days.code:
items = []
items.append('Gold')
print(items)
days.explanation: .append() is your best friend. It pushes new data to the end of the line. Your list grows as your data grows.

days.title: The Loop of Infinity 🔄
days.hook: Don't repeat yourself. Let Python do it.
days.code:
for i in range(3):
    print('Hello')
days.explanation: Loops repeat code! The range(3) makes it run three times — like hitting replay on your code.

days.title: Scanning Data Like a Pro 🕵️
days.hook: How does Instagram show all your photos?
days.explanation: For loops can walk through each item in a list — super handy for working with collections!

days.title: The Loop That NEVER Ends ⚠️
days.hook: Warning: This loop could crash your computer.
days.code:
x = 1
while x < 4:
    print(x)
    x += 1
days.explanation: While loops run until the condition becomes false. It’s like saying — keep going until you’re done!

days.title: Defining Your Own Powers 🦸
days.hook: You can invent your own Python commands.
days.explanation: Functions are reusable blocks of code! Define once, call anytime — just like shortcuts for your logic.

days.title: Functions Level 2: Arguments ⚔️
days.hook: Make your functions smarter.
days.explanation: We passed 'Bro' INTO the function. It's like feeding a machine. Input goes in, action comes out.

days.title: The Function Payback 💰
days.hook: Functions can give you things back.
days.explanation: Return sends data back from a function. Think of it like answering a question — you send the result back!

days.title: Lazy Coding Hacks (Defaults) 😴
days.hook: Too lazy to type? Python has your back.
days.explanation: Default parameters kick in when no value is given — super useful for quick function calls.

days.title: Slicing Data Ninja Style 🗡️
days.hook: Grab exactly what you want from any list.
days.code:
nums = [1,2,3,4,5]
print(nums[1:4])
days.explanation: Slicing helps you grab parts of a list — from index 1 up to (but not including) 4.

days.title: Tuples in Python
days.code:
colors = ('red', 'green', 'blue')
print(colors[0])
days.explanation: Tuples are like lists but can’t be changed. Perfect for data you want to keep safe.

days.title: Dictionaries
days.code:
person = {'name': 'Alex', 'age': 25}
print(person['name'])
days.explanation: Dictionaries store data in key-value pairs — just like a contact book where each name has info!

days.title: Adding to a Dictionary
days.explanation: You can easily add new key-value pairs — dictionaries are super flexible!

days.title: If in a List
days.explanation: You can check if an item exists using ‘in’. It’s a quick way to search through lists.

days.title: Nested Loops
days.explanation: Loops inside loops — perfect for grids, patterns, or combinations!

days.title: Break Statement
days.explanation: Break stops the loop early — great when you’ve found what you were looking for!

days.title: Continue Statement
days.explanation: Continue skips the current loop step — like saying ‘skip this one, move on!’

days.title: Nested If
days.explanation: You can put if statements inside each other — that’s called nesting. Super handy for detailed checks!

days.title: Bulletproof Code (Try/Except) 🛡️
days.hook: Stop your code from crashing.
days.explanation: You tried to divide by zero! That crashes programs. But 'Except' catches the crash and handles it safely.

days.title: Mini Project: Greeting App
days.code:
name = input('Your name: ')
print(f'Welcome to Python, {name}!')
days.explanation: Congrats! You’ve learned the basics. This simple app greets the user — and shows how input, variables, and f-strings come together!

days.title: Looping Smart with Counters 🫪e
days.hook: Stop counting manually like a caveman.
days.explanation: Enumerate gives you the item AND its position. No more manually counting i = 0 then i += 1!

days.title: Zipping Lists Together 🤐
days.hook: Two lists? One loop. Watch this.
days.code:
names = ['Leo', 'Neo']
powers = ['Fire', 'Ice']
for n, p in zip(names, powers):
    print(f'{n} uses {p}')
days.output:
Leo uses Fire
Neo uses Ice
days.explanation: Zip stitches two lists together like a zipper! Perfect for pairing data without complex loops.

days.title: The Duplicate Killer (Sets) 🚫
days.hook: Remove duplicates in ONE line of code.
days.code:
ids = [1, 2, 2, 3]
unique_ids = set(ids)
print(unique_ids)
days.explanation: Sets are collections that refuse duplicates. Convert a list to a set, and all repeats vanish instantly!

days.title: Venna Diagram Logic (Set Ops) 🥨
days.hook: Find common items between two groups instantly.
days.explanation: The & operator finds the intersection—items that exist in BOTH sets. It’s math magic in Python!

days.title: One-Line Loop Magic ✨
days.hook: Write a 3-line loop in 1 line.
days.code:
nums = [1, 2, 3]
squared = [x**2 for x in nums]
print(squared)
days.explanation: List comprehensions let you run a loop inside a list! It’s cleaner, faster, and makes you look like a pro.

days.title: The Code That Never Dies (Finally) 🧹
days.hook: Make sure your code cleans up its mess.
days.explanation: Finally runs no matter what—error or no error. It’s perfect for cleaning up messes or closing files.

days.title: Stopping the Chaos (Raise Exception) 🛑
days.hook: You can force your program to crash. Here is why.
days.explanation: Sometimes you WANT your program to crash if data is bad. Raise lets you trigger your own errors!

days.title: Unlimited Arguments (*args) 🌌
days.hook: Pass 1, 10, or 100 arguments. No errors.
days.explanation: The * lets you pass as many arguments as you want! Python packs them all into a tuple called nums.

days.title: Labelled Arguments (**kwargs) 🏷️
days.hook: Getting messy with arguments? Label them!
days.explanation: The ** gathers named arguments into a dictionary. It’s perfect for functions that need flexibility!

days.title: Anonymous Functions (Lambda) 👻
days.hook: A function with no name? Spooky.
days.code:
square = lambda x: x ** 2
print(square(5))
days.explanation: Lambdas are tiny, one-line functions without a name. Great for quick math or sorting tricks!

days.title: Math Power Unlimited ♾️
days.hook: Your calculator is jealous.
days.code:
import math
print(math.sqrt(16))
print(math.ceil(2.1))
days.explanation: Math module is your scientific calculator. Square roots, trigonometry, rounding—it’s all there!

days.title: Let Fate Decide (Random) 🎲
days.hook: Can't decide what to eat? Let Python pick.
days.code:
import random
choices = ['Pizza', 'Sushi']
print(random.choice(choices))
days.explanation: Can't decide? Let Python pick! random.choice is perfect for games, lotteries, or dinner plans.

days.title: Making Python Wait (Time) ⏳
days.hook: Add suspense to your code.
days.code:
import time
print('Loading...')
time.sleep(2)
print('Done!')
days.explanation: time.sleep() pauses your program. Essential for dramatic effect, countdowns, or waiting for data.

days.title: What Time Is It? ⌚
days.hook: Track the exact moment in time.
days.code:
import datetime
now = datetime.datetime.now()
print(now.strftime('%H:%M:%S'))
days.explanation: Computers live by the clock. datetime lets you grab the exact moment, format it, and track time.

days.title: Hacking Your File System (OS) 📂
days.hook: Control your computer's folders with code.
days.code:
import os
print(os.getcwd())
days.explanation: The OS module lets you talk to your operating system. Find out where you are, list files, or make folders!

days.title: Saving Your Secrets (Write) 💾
days.hook: Save your data so it doesn't vanish.
days.code:
file = open('secret.txt', 'w')
file.write('This is hidden.')
file.close()
days.explanation: Programs usually forget everything when they stop. Writing to a file saves your data FOREVER (until you delete it).

days.title: Reading Your Secrets Back 📖
days.hook: Read the file you just saved.
days.code:
file = open('secret.txt', 'r')
content = file.read()
print(content)
file.close()
days.explanation: Reading brings data back into your program. Only existing files can be read, or Python crashes!

days.title: The Never-Ending Log (Append) 📝
days.hook: Add to a file without deleting the old stuff.
days.code:
file = open('log.txt', 'a')
file.write('New entry!\n')
file.close()
days.explanation: Start mode 'w' wipes the file clean. Mode 'a' (append) adds to the end—perfect for diaries or logs!

days.title: The Safety Net (With Open) 🛡️
days.hook: Never forget to close a file again.
days.code:
with open('data.txt', 'w') as f:
    f.write('Safe!')
# File closes automatically here
days.explanation: Forget file.close()? Python has your back. The with block closes the file automatically, even if errors happen!

days.title: Saving Complicated Data (JSON) 📦
days.hook: Save entire lists and dictionaries easily.
days.code:
import json
data = {'name': 'Max', 'level': 5}
saved = json.dumps(data)
print(saved)
days.explanation: Text files are boring. JSON lets you save Lists and Dictionaries as text, then load them back perfectly!

days.title: Your First Blueprint (Class) 🏗️
days.hook: Build your own custom objects.
days.code:
class Robot:
    pass

my_bot = Robot()
print(my_bot)
days.explanation: A Class is a blueprint. An Object is the thing you build from it. We just built an empty robot!

days.title: Bringing It To Life (__init__) ⚡
days.hook: Give your objects a soul.
days.explanation: The __init__ function runs AUTOMATICALLY when you create an object. It’s the spark of life!

days.title: Giving It Personality (Self) 🏷️
days.hook: Why do we use 'self' everywhere?
days.explanation: Self refers to the specific object being built. self.name attaches the name tag to THIS robot.

days.title: Teaching Robots Tricks (Methods) 🦾
days.hook: Make your objects DO things.
days.explanation: Methods are just functions inside a class. They let objects DO things, like speak or move.

days.title: Shared Knowledge (Class Vars) 🧠
days.hook: Variables shared by EVERY object.
days.code:
class Robot:
    laws = 3

a = Robot()
b = Robot()
print(a.laws, b.laws)
days.explanation: Class variables are shared by ALL objects. Every robot knows the 3 laws!

days.title: The Robot Family (Inheritance) 👨‍👦
days.hook: Code once, use everywhere.
days.explanation: Robots inherit from Machines! They get all the parent’s abilities for free. Less code, more power.

days.title: Rebelling Against Parents (Override) 🤘
days.hook: Change how inherited methods work.
days.explanation: Child classes can change how inherited methods work. A car starts differently than a washing machine!

days.title: Who Are You? (__str__) 🆔
days.hook: Make printing objects look beautiful.
days.code:
class Cat:
    def __str__(self): return 'Meow'

c = Cat()
print(c)
days.explanation: By default, printing an object looks ugly. __str__ lets you decide what an object looks like as text!

days.title: Making Your Own Tools (Modules) 🛠️
days.hook: Create your own 'import' commands.
days.code:
# In file bot.py
def speak(): print('Hi')

# In main.py
import bot
bot.speak()
days.explanation: Any Python file is a module! Save your functions in one file, import them in another. Organize your life.

days.title: Mini Project: RPG Character ⚔️
days.hook: Build a game character with stats.
days.code:
class Hero:
    def __init__(self, hp): self.hp = hp
    def heal(self): self.hp += 10

p = Hero(50)
p.heal()
print(p.hp)
days.explanation: You made a video game character! It has health, and it can heal. This is the power of OOP!

days.title: Recursion: Function Calling Itself
days.explanation: Recursion breaks a big problem into smaller versions of itself. Here factorial keeps reducing until the base case.

days.title: Binary Search in Minutes
days.explanation: Binary search cuts the search space in half each step, making lookup very fast on sorted data.

days.title: Custom Sorting with key
days.code:
words = ['python', 'ai', 'automation', 'ml']
print(sorted(words, key=len, reverse=True))
days.explanation: The key function tells Python what value to compare. Here we sort by length, not alphabetically.

days.title: Dictionary Comprehension Pro Move
days.explanation: Comprehensions build collections in a clean and expressive one-liner.

days.title: Generators Save Memory
days.explanation: Generators yield values lazily, so you do not store everything in memory at once.

days.title: Generator Delegation with yield from
days.explanation: yield from forwards values from another iterable, making generator composition simple.

days.title: Decorators: Upgrade Functions
days.explanation: Decorators wrap behavior around a function without changing its core code.

days.title: Parameterized Decorators
days.explanation: A decorator can accept arguments, so behavior becomes reusable and configurable.

days.title: Closures Keep State
days.code:
def counter():
    value = 0
    def inc():
        nonlocal value
        value += 1
        return value
    return inc

c = counter()
print(c(), c())
days.explanation: A closure remembers variables from its outer scope, even after that scope is finished.

days.title: map filter reduce Combo
days.code:
from functools import reduce
nums = [1, 2, 3, 4]
ev = list(filter(lambda x: x % 2 == 0, nums))
print(reduce(lambda a, b: a + b, ev))
days.explanation: Functional tools let you transform, filter, and aggregate data in pipeline style.

days.title: any and all for Fast Validation
days.code:
scores = [90, 82, 77]
print(all(s >= 70 for s in scores))
print(any(s > 95 for s in scores))
days.explanation: Use all for every-condition checks and any for at-least-one checks.

days.title: Advanced Unpacking
days.code:
first, *middle, last = [10, 20, 30, 40, 50]
print(first, middle, last)
days.explanation: Star unpacking captures flexible chunks from iterables.

days.title: Mastering args and kwargs
days.code:
def debug(*args, **kwargs):
    print(args)
    print(kwargs)

debug(1, 2, mode='fast', retry=3)
days.explanation: Use *args for positional flexibility and **kwargs for named flexibility.

days.title: Dataclasses for Clean Models
days.explanation: Dataclasses auto-generate init and repr, reducing boilerplate.

days.title: Properties for Controlled Access
days.explanation: Properties expose computed values like attributes while keeping logic inside class methods.

days.title: classmethod vs staticmethod
days.explanation: classmethod knows the class, staticmethod is just a namespaced utility function.

days.title: Custom Exceptions for Clear Errors
days.explanation: Custom exceptions communicate intent better than generic crashes.

days.title: Production Logging Basics
days.code:
import logging
logging.basicConfig(level=logging.INFO)
logging.info('Pipeline started')
days.explanation: Logging is better than print in real systems because levels and formatting are built in.

days.title: Pathlib Beats String Paths
days.code:
from pathlib import Path
p = Path('output') / 'video.mp4'
print(p.as_posix())
days.explanation: Pathlib gives cleaner, safer file path operations across operating systems.

days.title: Regex Quick Start
days.code:
import re
text = 'mail me at hi@test.com'
print(bool(re.search(r'\S+@\S+\.\S+', text)))
days.explanation: Regular expressions help match complex text patterns efficiently.

days.title: Regex Groups Extraction
days.code:
import re
m = re.search(r'(\d{4})-(\d{2})-(\d{2})', '2026-04-10')
print(m.groups())
days.explanation: Groups let you capture specific pieces from a matched pattern.

days.title: Build a Context Manager Class
days.explanation: Context managers guarantee setup and cleanup around critical code blocks.

days.title: contextlib Shortcut
days.code:
from contextlib import contextmanager

@contextmanager
def section(name):
    print(f'BEGIN {name}')
    yield
    print(f'END {name}')

with section('ETL'):
    print('running')
days.explanation: contextmanager decorator creates context managers without writing a full class.

days.title: Type Hints That Scale
days.explanation: Type hints improve readability and tooling for larger codebases.

days.title: Abstract Base Classes
days.explanation: ABCs define contracts so implementations stay consistent.

days.title: Unit Testing with unittest
days.explanation: Automated tests catch regressions before they hit production.

days.title: Testing Exceptions
days.explanation: Good tests verify both expected behavior and expected failures.

days.title: Mocking External Calls
days.code:
from unittest.mock import patch

with patch('random.randint', return_value=7):
    import random
    print(random.randint(1, 10))
days.explanation: Mocks isolate your tests from external randomness, APIs, and side effects.

days.title: Robust JSON Read Write
days.code:
import json
payload = {'ok': True, 'count': 3}
text = json.dumps(payload)
print(json.loads(text)['count'])
days.explanation: Serialize with dumps and deserialize with loads to move structured data safely.

days.title: CSV Processing Pipeline
days.code:
import csv
rows = [['name', 'score'], ['A', 90], ['B', 75]]
print(rows[1][0], rows[1][1])
days.explanation: CSV is still a major data format in analytics and automation pipelines.

days.title: SQLite Database Basics
days.code:
import sqlite3
conn = sqlite3.connect(':memory:')
cur = conn.cursor()
cur.execute('CREATE TABLE users(name TEXT)')
cur.execute('INSERT INTO users VALUES (?)', ('Hardik',))
print(cur.execute('SELECT COUNT(*) FROM users').fetchone()[0])
days.explanation: SQLite is perfect for local apps, prototyping, and lightweight persistence.

days.title: Transactions and Rollback
days.code:
import sqlite3
conn = sqlite3.connect(':memory:')
cur = conn.cursor()
cur.execute('CREATE TABLE t(v INT)')
conn.execute('BEGIN')
cur.execute('INSERT INTO t VALUES (1)')
conn.rollback()
print(cur.execute('SELECT COUNT(*) FROM t').fetchone()[0])
days.explanation: Transactions ensure all-or-nothing safety for critical data changes.

days.title: Priority Queue with heapq
days.code:
import heapq
q = []
heapq.heappush(q, (2, 'low'))
heapq.heappush(q, (1, 'high'))
print(heapq.heappop(q)[1])
days.explanation: Heaps always pop the smallest priority first, ideal for schedulers and pathfinding.

days.title: Counter and defaultdict
days.code:
from collections import Counter, defaultdict

print(Counter('banana')['a'])

d = defaultdict(int)
d['x'] += 1
print(d['x'])
days.explanation: collections module gives battle-tested data structures for common patterns.

days.title: Sliding Window with deque
days.code:
from collections import deque

window = deque(maxlen=3)
for x in [1, 2, 3, 4]:
    window.append(x)

print(list(window))
days.explanation: deque with maxlen is perfect for rolling buffers and streaming metrics.

days.title: Binary Insert with bisect
days.code:
import bisect
arr = [1, 3, 8]
bisect.insort(arr, 5)
print(arr)
days.explanation: bisect keeps sorted lists ordered with logarithmic search positioning.

days.title: itertools Product and Permutations
days.code:
from itertools import product, permutations
print(list(product([1,2], ['A'])))
print(len(list(permutations([1,2,3], 2))))
days.explanation: itertools helps generate combinations efficiently without manual nested loops.

days.title: Grouping with itertools.groupby
days.code:
from itertools import groupby
items = ['a', 'a', 'b', 'b', 'b']
for k, g in groupby(items):
    print(k, len(list(g)))
days.explanation: groupby summarizes consecutive runs, useful after sorting by key.

days.title: Memoization with lru_cache
days.explanation: Caching saves repeated computation and can turn slow recursion into fast solutions.

days.title: Dynamic Programming Starter
days.explanation: Dynamic programming stores subproblem results to avoid recalculating work.

days.title: Two Pointers Pattern
days.code:
s = 'level'
l, r = 0, len(s) - 1
ok = True

while l < r:
    if s[l] != s[r]:
        ok = False
        break
    l += 1
    r -= 1

print(ok)
days.explanation: Two pointers solve many array and string tasks in linear time.

days.title: Stack Pattern: Valid Parentheses
days.explanation: Stacks track nested structure and are essential for parser-like problems.

days.title: Queue Pattern: BFS
days.explanation: BFS explores level by level and is core to shortest path in unweighted graphs.

days.title: Depth First Search
days.explanation: DFS goes deep before backtracking and appears in trees, graphs, and backtracking tasks.

days.title: Dijkstra Shortest Path
days.explanation: Dijkstra finds shortest paths on weighted graphs with non-negative edges.

days.title: Binary Tree Traversal
days.explanation: Tree traversals are foundational for hierarchical data processing.

days.title: Merge Intervals
days.explanation: Interval merging is a classic real-world pattern for schedules and ranges.

days.title: Top K Elements with Heap
days.code:
import heapq
nums = [9, 1, 7, 3, 10]
print(heapq.nlargest(3, nums))
days.explanation: Heaps extract top-k efficiently without full sorting.

days.title: Asyncio First Step
days.code:
import asyncio

async def main():
    print('start')
    await asyncio.sleep(0.1)
    print('done')

asyncio.run(main())
days.explanation: Async lets a program handle many waiting tasks efficiently in one thread.

days.title: Run Tasks Concurrently with gather
days.code:
import asyncio

async def work(n):
    await asyncio.sleep(0.1)
    return n


async def main():
    results = await asyncio.gather(work(1), work(2))
    print(results)


asyncio.run(main())
days.explanation: gather schedules multiple coroutines and waits for all results together.

days.title: Async Timeout Protection
days.code:
import asyncio

async def slow():
    await asyncio.sleep(1)

async def main():
    try:
        await asyncio.wait_for(slow(), timeout=0.1)
    except asyncio.TimeoutError:
        print('timed out')

asyncio.run(main())
days.explanation: Timeouts prevent your async workflow from hanging forever on slow operations.

days.title: Threading with Queue
days.code:
from queue import Queue
from threading import Thread
q = Queue()

def worker():
    print(q.get())
    q.task_done()

Thread(target=worker).start()
q.put('job-1')
days.explanation: Queue makes thread communication safer than sharing mutable data directly.

days.title: Multiprocessing for CPU Work
days.code:
from multiprocessing import Pool

def square(x): return x*x

with Pool(2) as p:
    print(p.map(square, [2,3,4]))
days.explanation: Use processes for CPU-heavy tasks to bypass the GIL and scale better.

days.title: HTTP Requests with urllib
days.code:
from urllib.parse import urlencode
params = urlencode({'q': 'python'})
print(f'https://example.com/search?{params}')
days.explanation: Even with stdlib, you can build robust URLs and request workflows.

days.title: Retry with Exponential Backoff
days.code:
import time
for attempt in range(3):
    wait = 2 ** attempt
    print(f'attempt {attempt+1}, wait {wait}s')
    time.sleep(0)
days.output:
attempt 1, wait 1s
attempt 2, wait 2s
attempt 3, wait 4s
days.explanation: Backoff retries reduce pressure on flaky services and improve reliability.

days.title: Professional CLI with argparse
days.code:
import argparse
parser = argparse.ArgumentParser()
parser.add_argument('--name')
args = parser.parse_args(['--name', 'Hardik'])
print(args.name)
days.explanation: argparse turns scripts into proper command-line tools.

days.title: Package Your Code Cleanly
days.code:
# mypkg/__init__.py
# mypkg/core.py
def run():
    return 'ready'

print(run())
days.explanation: Organizing code into packages keeps projects maintainable as they grow.

days.title: Mini Project: API Client Class
days.code:
class APIClient:
    def __init__(self, base): self.base = base
    def endpoint(self, path): return f'{self.base}/{path}'

client = APIClient('https://api.site.com')
print(client.endpoint('health'))
days.explanation: Create reusable client abstractions for cleaner integration code.

days.title: Mini Project: Task Scheduler Core
days.code:
tasks = []

def add_task(name, prio):
    tasks.append((prio, name))


add_task('render', 2)
add_task('upload', 1)
print(sorted(tasks))
days.explanation: A small scheduler combines data structures, sorting, and system design thinking.

days.title: Final Project: CLI Data Pipeline
days.code:
def extract(): return [1,2,3]
def transform(data): return [x*10 for x in data]
def load(data): print(data)

load(transform(extract()))
days.explanation: You now combine modular design, transformations, and execution flow like a real engineering pipeline.
